title: Chapter 20: The Convergence
content: I hand him the phone.

Julian takes it the way he takes everything — with the easy confidence of a man who's never been handed something he couldn't understand. His thumb finds the screen. The Fork app glows: three paths, three labels, three lives I've been living in the spaces between this one.

His expression moves through stages like weather over Manhattan — confusion first, a slight narrowing of the eyes; then recognition, the moment he sees the word Paris and something connects; then disbelief, his jaw slackening half a centimeter, the most emotion I've ever seen him wear in a boardroom or a bedroom or anywhere in between.
"What is this?"

"I don't know," I say. My voice is steadier than I expect. "It appeared on my phone the night of graduation. I didn't download it. It can't be deleted. And it —" I swallow. "It lets me live other lives."

I wait for him to call security. To suggest a psych evaluation. To do the math on how many red flags I just triggered and politely, professionally distance himself from the woman who just confessed to interdimensional app usage on a Tuesday evening. I've rehearsed this moment a hundred ways, and in every version, it ends with him walking out the door.
He doesn't walk out the door.

The silence stretches — five seconds, ten, long enough that I can hear the radiator clicking in the corner and the distant wail of a siren on the street below and my own heartbeat doing something arrhythmic against my ribs.
Then he says: "Show me."
Two words. No qualifiers. No are you sure or maybe we should think about this. Just show me, the same way he says acquire them or close the deal — clean, imperative, absolute.
I press the Paris path.
His hand is on my arm — he reached for me without thinking, some instinct older than Wall Street, older than caution, and the contact is warm and solid and real.
And the world folds.

It doesn't shift the way it does when I'm alone. Alone, the transition is a ripple, a drop through water, a held breath and a release. This is a convulsion. The apartment compresses around us like a fist, the walls rushing in and then dissolving, and I feel Julian's grip tighten on my arm until his fingers are bone and pressure and the only anchor in the spinning. There's sound — a frequency I can't name, like tinnitus cranked to a roar — and taste, copper and ozone flooding my mouth, and the smell of everything burning and blooming at once.
Then light.

Golden, thick, pouring through tall windows I don't recognize. The quality of it is wrong for Manhattan — too soft, too old, slanting across hardwood floors at an angle that speaks of a different latitude entirely. The air tastes different. Wine and rain and some faint perfume I know is mine but isn't — rose absolute, the one I bought at a marché aux puces in a life that isn't this one.
We're standing in the middle of Timeline B Avery's apartment.
Julian releases my arm. He steps forward — not reeling, not staggering, just moving toward the window like a man drawn by gravity. He looks out at the Seine.
The light catches his face, and his face does something I've never seen.
It breaks.

Not dramatically. Not the way it does in movies — no gasping, no trembling. A hairline fracture in the professional mask, so fine you'd miss it if you weren't watching. His shoulders drop a centimeter. His mouth softens. His eyes — those calculating, silver-gray eyes — go wide and defenseless and young.
"This is real," he says.
Not a question.
"This is actually real."

He turns to look at me, and the light from the Seine-window falls on him sideways, and I see it. The same look Julian B gave me at the gallery — that collision of recognition and gravity, like a man who's been falling toward something his whole life and just realized what it is. The same man. Meeting the same woman. For the first time — again.
My chest cracks open.

I want to say something. I want to say I'm sorry and I should have told you and I didn't know how and none of the words are big enough. I want to touch him and confirm he's solid, that he made it through, that I didn't just break reality with a man who trusts me enough to say show me without asking a single follow-up question.
I don't get the chance.
My phone detonates.

Not literally — but the vibration is so violent I nearly drop it, and the screen is a cascade of red, notification after notification stacking like a car pileup, three urgent banners fighting for the same surface. The sound is a triple-tone alarm I've never heard before, high and insistent and wrong, and it fills the Paris apartment with a sense of emergency that doesn't belong here, that doesn't belong anywhere, that belongs everywhere at once.
⚠️ EMERGENCY — HARKER HAS LOCATED TARGET IN CORK CITY. TIMELINE C.
⚠️ CELESTE KNOWS — SHE'S GOING PUBLIC ABOUT YOUR RELATIONSHIP WITH J. DEVERAUX. TIMELINE B.
⚠️ STERLING VP HARVEY HAS FILED A COMPLAINT AGAINST YOU — ACCUSATION: CORPORATE ESPIONAGE. TIMELINE A.
Three timelines. Three crises. All at once.

I read them twice because my brain is trying to reject the input. Harker found Julian C in Cork — Harker, who collects debts in broken fingers and hollowed-out lives. Celeste — Julian B's ex, the gallery owner, the one with the press connections and the vendetta — she knows about us, and she's going to burn it all public. And Harvey — Sterling's VP, the man who's been circling me since my first week — he's filed a complaint. Corporate espionage. Career death sentence.

The room spins. No — the room is fine. I'm spinning. Or reality is spinning. Or all three realities are spinning on the same axis and I'm standing at the center, pinned like a specimen, watching all of them come apart.
Julian is already processing.

I watch it happen in real time — his face reassembles, the fractures sealed, the professional mask clicking back into place like armor. Wall Street Julian. Crisis Julian. The man who buys and sells companies before breakfast and doesn't flinch when the market crashes. He crosses to me, takes the phone from my shaking hand, reads all three notifications in one quick scan.
"Prioritize," he says. Voice steady. "Which one is life or death?"

He doesn't ask if this is real. He doesn't ask how a phone can receive notifications from multiple timelines. He doesn't waste a single second on the questions that would paralyze anyone else. He just — prioritizes. Like it's a hostile takeover. Like we're in a war room. Like the multiverse is just another spreadsheet and he's going to find the cell that's on fire.
And God, I could kiss him for that.

Instead I think about Julian C. Julian Blake. The Julian who runs a bookstore in Kinsale and owes money to a man named Harker who doesn't issue warnings — only consequences. I think about the weight of that debt and the kind of person Harker is and the fact that located target doesn't mean Harker is coming — it means Harker is already there.
"Ireland," I say. "I have to go to Ireland."
Julian grabs my arm.
"Not alone."

He means it. I can see it in the set of his jaw, the tension in his grip, the way his whole body angles toward me like a shield. This man — this man from Wall Street, from boardrooms and corner offices and a world where the worst thing that can happen is a quarterly loss — he's ready to follow me into a world where people carry guns and debts are paid in blood. He doesn't even hesitate.
My throat closes.
"Julian —"
"Whatever's happening in Ireland, I'm going with you."
"You can't."
"I just traveled through — whatever that was — to get to Paris." He gestures at the window, the Seine, the impossible golden light. "You think I'm going to let you face something worse alone?"

"The app only shifts my consciousness." The words come out rough. "You came through because you were touching me — I didn't know that was even possible, and I can't guarantee it'll happen again, and I can't —" My voice breaks. "I can't guarantee your safety in a world with Harker. I can't guarantee you'd even arrive in the right place. I can't guarantee anything."
I touch his face.

The same face. The same gray eyes. The same bones, the same jaw, the same man I've been falling in love with across three different lives and now I'm standing in Paris with the Wall Street version and he's looking at me like I'm the only real thing in a world that just proved it isn't, and I have to leave him.
"I have to do this alone."

His jaw tightens. I feel the muscle shift under my palm. He doesn't like it — every instinct in his body is rejecting it, I can tell, the same instincts that make him a predator in negotiations, the ones that say never yield the advantage, never walk away from a fight, never let anyone you —
He nods.
Once. Short. Definitive.
"Then come back."

Three words. A command and a prayer. The most Julian A sentence that has ever been spoken — not I love you, not please be careful, not any of the soft things other men might say. Then come back. Imperative. Absolute. Like he's daring the universe to keep me.

I press my forehead to his for one second. One. I breathe him in — cedar and vetiver and the faint coffee he was drinking before I handed him my phone and shattered everything he thought he knew. His hand comes up to the back of my neck, warm and steady.
I shift.

The world folds harder this time. No — not folds. Crumples. Like paper in a fist. Like a heart in a chest. Like everything solid and sequential and sane being wadded up and thrown across a room. The Paris apartment shrieks away from me — the golden light, the smell of rain on the Seine, the warmth of Julian's hand — and I'm falling through a place that has no name, a between-space where I can hear all three timelines humming at different frequencies, three heartbeats in three chests and they're all mine and they're all breaking.

I aim for base reality. Timeline A. Home. Because Timeline C — Ireland, Cork, Harker, Julian C — is accessible only from there, and I need to go through the door to get to the room where someone might be dying.
I land in my apartment.
Or what's left of it.

The glitching is catastrophic. The walls are gone — not missing, not destroyed, just — not. Where the east wall should be, I see the Paris apartment, the Seine glinting through the window, the golden light pooling on hardwood floors that exist in a country I'm not currently standing in. Where the south wall should be, I see Cork Harbor, gunmetal water and fishing boats and the smell of the Irish Sea hitting me like a fist — salt and seaweed and diesel and something underneath, something iron-sharp, blood-sharp. Where the west wall should be, I see the Sterling boardroom, all glass and steel and cold fluorescent light, and Harvey's face frozen mid-accusation on a screen I can't reach.
Three worlds. One room. All of them bleeding into each other.

I stumble. My hand finds the edge of the kitchen counter — or tries to. My fingers pass through it. For one blood-freezing second I can see my own bones, my own knuckles, translucent and flickering, the counter visible through my skin like I'm a bad projection, a signal losing resolution.

My phone is in my hand. I didn't grab it. It was in Paris. But it's here now, because I guess objects belong to the consciousness that carries them and mine is the only consciousness insane enough to be carrying anything through this.
The screen glows red.
INTEGRITY: 31%
⚠️ CRITICAL INTEGRITY FAILURE. TIMELINE COLLAPSE IMMINENT.
Thirty-one percent. The number sears itself into my vision. Below it, smaller text scrolls: Structural cohesion declining. Timeline boundaries unstable. Recommend immediate —

The recommendation cuts off. The screen fills with static. The phone vibrates once, hard, like it's trying to shake loose from my grip, and I clutch it tighter because it's the only tether I have and —
A sound.
Not from the phone. From everywhere. From the walls-that-aren't-walls, from the overlapping realities bleeding into each other like watercolors in rain. A voice.
Not Julian A.
Not Julian B.
Not Julian C.
All three.
"Avery."

One word, three voices, speaking in perfect unison — the crisp Wall Street baritone and the warm Parisian murmur and the rough Irish brogue layered on top of each other like a chord, like a harmony, like something that should not be possible and is happening anyway, reverberating through the collapsed architecture of a room that exists in three places at once.
"Don't let go."

The three timelines shudder. The Paris window flickers. The Cork Harbor darkens. The boardroom screen cracks down the middle. I can feel all three of them — the weight of the golden light on my skin, the salt-wind cutting through my hair, the air-conditioning drying my eyes — overlapping, conflicting, threatening to tear me apart like a current pulling in three directions.

I'm standing in the ruins of everything I've built, and the bill for my double-living is due, and the only thing keeping me from being scattered across three realities like ashes in the wind is the sound of his voice — their voices — saying my name like a door I can still walk through if I just —
If I just don't let go.
INTEGRITY: 29%.

The number drops. The walls flicker. The sound of the sea gets louder, and the golden light gets brighter, and somewhere in Cork a man named Harker is closing in on a man named Julian who said I love you in a bookshop and meant it, and somewhere in Paris a woman named Celeste is loading a tweet that will burn my other life to ash, and somewhere in Manhattan a vice president named Harvey is signing his name to a complaint that will end my career, and I cannot save all of them.
I cannot save all of them.
But I can try.

I close my eyes. I feel the phone in my hand — solid, real, the only object that exists in all three worlds at once. I feel the warmth of Julian A's hand where it gripped my arm ten seconds or ten hours ago. I feel the echo of all three voices, the chord they made, the way they said my name like a lifeline thrown across dimensions.
I plant my feet on the floor that might not be there.
I hold on.
And I choose.
